nativeRange.habitat: Surge-swept reef fronts and submarine terraces, typically perched among branches of live stony corals (Pocillopora, Stylophora, Acropora).

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and ledges for perching
branching stony coral (Pocillopora, Stylophora, Acropora) where reef-keeping allows

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty marine foods including mysis shrimp, frozen marine preparations, chopped shrimp/fish, and quality pellets. Has been reported to readily take prepared foods soon after acclimation.

avoidWith: ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, sexy, anemone shrimp)
conspecifics in anything but a very large system

summary: Small, brilliant-red hawkfish that perches in branching corals and rockwork. Generally considered reef-safe with corals but is known to predate ornamental shrimp and small hermit crabs.
careNotes:
The Flame Hawkfish is one of the most popular small hawkfishes in the marine trade, prized for its bright cherry-red body and contrasting black dorsal markings. It is generally regarded as hardy and adaptable, which is part of why sources tend to call its care level easy/beginner despite it being a marine fish.

Like other hawkfishes it lacks a swim bladder and spends most of its time perched on rockwork or in the branches of live stony corals (Pocillopora, Stylophora, Acropora) waiting for prey to pass. A structured reef aquascape with elevated perches and branching coral or coral-skeleton equivalents is strongly recommended; in a bare or low-structure tank it has been reported to look stressed and remain unsettled.

Reef caveat: it is generally considered reef-safe with corals, but it is an opportunistic micro-carnivore that has been documented preying on ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, sexy, anemone shrimp) and small hermit crabs. Keepers who want to maintain a cleanup crew with ornamental shrimp should generally avoid this species.

Temperament toward fish is usually peaceful-to-semi-aggressive: it tends to tolerate larger, faster tankmates but may harass small bottom-dwellers (tiny gobies, dartfish) and other hawkfishes. It is best kept singly unless the tank is very large and a confirmed mated pair is introduced together.

Min tank size: retailers commonly list 30 US gal (~114 L) which has been rounded up to a standard 120 L footprint here; long, wide tanks with horizontal swimming and perching room are preferred over tall ones. Sources broadly agree on temperature 22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, and specific gravity 1.020–1.025; FishBase gives a max length of 9 cm TL and retailers commonly list ~4 in (10 cm), so 9–10 cm is the working adult range.
breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; not commonly bred in home aquaria. Captive spawning events have been reported only in very large, well-established systems with established pairs, and rearing of the planktonic larvae is generally regarded as difficult and not routinely accomplished in the hobby.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; larval rearing has rarely been documented in home aquaria.
